Transcrição Completa: 84 Messaging Mastery 7 13 24
[00:00] All right. So, this came up a few different times this week, so I wanted to make sure that we were able to cover it collectively for the group on a weekly call. This is a recurring lesson that seems to continuously come up for a lot of people. They I I'm I'm assuming are just struggling with how to think with it.
[00:18] So, rather than just talking on the tactics, I'm going to mix in both. I'm going I'm going to talk about the how to think with it and then I'm also going to give some tactical examples and we use the people on the call here to just give some of that perspective.
[00:32] And to be clear, today's topic once again messaging mastery. So messaging in its entirety controls who is probable to respond to our advertising efforts.
[00:43] And messaging applies to every asset that exists that's customer-facing. It exists in our ads from our body copy to what we say inside of our videos or what we express inside of our images.
[00:56] It obviously applies on funnels from the VSSLs on the pages to the headlines to all the minute little body copy below it. It applies in the marketing automation email followup that goes out to people, the text follow-up.
[01:10] In addition to that, it applies to the salespeople as well for how the salespeople are actually communicating. So, to be clear, allow me to share my screen here and just give you guys some visuals on this so we're all on the same page.
[01:24] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Messaging Mastery - Who You're Going After"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Apresentação inicial sobre estrutura de messaging, foco em identificação de público-alvo
[01:28] The important thing that I need you to just understand initially is who specifically you're going after and once again how you're going to go about communicating with them.
[01:38] So, we were we were going to launch a new client. His name's Alex and Alex is a trader. To be clear, Alex has a challenge that he does where he turns $100 into a million dollars. That's the challenge goal.
[01:52] The last time that he did this, he turned $100 into, believe it or not, a little over $379,000, I think was the exact number. And it was crazy.
[02:03] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Alex - Trader Case Study"
NÚMEROS: $100 → $379,000+, Curso: $1,500-$3,000, Desafio: 3 meses
VISUAL: Exemplo de caso de trading, demonstração de transformação financeira, estrutura de oferta
[02:06] So turning that amount of money into that, you know, big of a sum, first of all, is pretty extreme. And not a lot of people can do that. But when you hear that right off the bat, one thing that you're likely going to consider is who well, who's that's pro who is that probable to attract just right off the bat.
[02:25] Okay. So once again, if I have a guy who's turning $100 and the goal is to turn it into a million, his course that he sells is anywhere from $1,500 to $3,000. Okay?
[02:36] And once again, when you when you initially just talk about, you know, taking only a hundred bucks and turning it into over a million, I mean, you can you can bet right off the rip, there's going to be a substantial quantity of people that are attracted to it that just, you know, are going to be in a in an inferior financial position, okay?
[02:55] That won't have a high probability to be able to eventually go on afford the the offer that the guy has. And once again, the offer being a $1,500 to, you know, $3,000 course.
[03:05] Now, if they follow along and they actually take the guy's trades, I mean, sure, they'll have the cash to be able to do it. This guy's a crazy successful trader to be clear.
[03:14] And although right off the bat, I mean, you also get a lot of skepticism that comes from that that level of claim, you know, like naturally you're going to get a lot of people that are just like, I mean, there's no way that you're going to be able to turn $100 into a million dollar.
[03:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Challenge Results & Skepticism"
NÚMEROS: 38,000 registros no webinar, $7 milhões em vendas, Centenas de milhares de visualizações
VISUAL: Métricas de conversão do desafio, demonstração de ceticismo vs. resultados documentados
[03:31] And to actually reiterate this, believe it or not, I actually get targeted with a tweet. I'm just scrolling through Twitter and I see a I see a random tweet from a guy who is also a successful trader and you know he's highly skeptical of this guy's claim even though there's documented proof of it.
[03:47] I mean the guy does a daily live video recording going through each trade that he takes live, you know, he's not like pre-recording it and then posting it after. He'll like live stream the whole thing and then he turns those into shorts and then he also has like a long form piece of content that he does on a weekly basis to review the trades.
[04:07] So although it's all documented, to be clear, this claim introduces a substantial amount of skepticism right off the bat. Okay? It attracts a low quality when it comes to financially qualification individual.
[04:20] It attracts a lot of people who want to do the get-rich-quick thing. And in addition to that, it just right off the rip causes an excessive amount of skepticism.
[04:29] However, because he was able last time to turn $100 into a little over $379,000 and he did it, this is the craziest part, right? The challenges within three months. So, he does swing trading like that's his specific strategy.
[04:44] And there were without exaggeration hundreds of thousands of people who saw this initial piece of content. So this being like the front end, you know, what gets the attention after the challenge was technically completed.
[04:57] The next step down was, hey, I got this webinar where where I'll go in detail and show you the actual strategy that I did. And this guy had 38,000 people that registered for his webinar.
[05:10] Okay. Out of those 38,000 people, I don't remember the exact amount of buyers. However, I know the exact amount that the guy made off of it, which was insane. This guy made $7 million off of those 38,000 webinar registrants.
[05:26] And that was that was off of the $3,000 course to be clear, just to give some perspective. And in addition to that, the guy has some upsells. Like there's things that he'll sell into the future.
[05:36] However, I don't know all the exact numbers on the next steps, but simply put, there's additional revenue that'll come from that as a result because the guy's got upsells that people can go through. Okay?
[05:46] So, the moral of the story is this guy has a lot of money coming in from this webinar that he does. Okay? And long story short, although yeah, he's selling a $3,000 offer and he doesn't say that on the front end.
[05:59] And there's a lot of skepticism that naturally naturally comes as a result of that claim of turning $100 into a million dollars. And yeah, he technically fell short. He only turned $100 into $379,000.
[06:13] There were still hundreds of thousands of people that followed along. And it was just a numbers game that in in his case successfully played out and worked well for him.
[06:23] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Low Probability vs. High Articulation"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Comparação entre abordagem de volume vs. abordagem estratégica de messaging, contraste entre sucesso orgânico e publicidade paga
[06:25] There's an extremely uncommon low probability for it to play out from an advertising perspective and you know from an organic perspective for most brands and businesses.
[06:36] What most brands and businesses have to do is they have to be able to articulate themselves much better and much more articulate on the front end of these offers.
[06:45] They have to be able to communicate successfully to key demographics that they're attempting to convert.
[06:51] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Andrew - Amazon Course Case Study"
NÚMEROS: $5,000-$10,000 investimento necessário
VISUAL: Exemplo de cliente Inner Circle, problema de qualificação de leads, feedback de vendedores
[06:53] Yesterday I was on a call with one of the inner circle members. His name's Andrew. Andrew has an Amazon course and he was seeing a lower quantity of people converting month over month.
[07:04] In addition to that, sales people continuously were giving feedback that they were getting a lot of people coming in that were unqualified and that just, you know, once again, salespeople in this case were being honest and accurate with their feedback.
[07:18] And they were just saying, "Look, people are coming in not as financially qualified, not as motivated to purchase, you know, not as uh not as capable financially for being able to get into the to the Amazon offer."
[07:32] And you go and you look at a lot of his ads and none of them spoke to the right type of people. Not a single ad, not a not a single VSSL on the funnel, not a single word on the funnel was geared towards a person who was in a specific condition.
[07:48] And you go to ask the guy, you're like, well, what is what is the ideal situation here? Who's the right type of person? You know, where where are they at in life? What do they got going on for himself?
[08:00] So, he starts breaking down his ideal demographic. And this is a great place to start to be clear is you want to start off with just once again what are the ideal demographics.
[08:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Ideal Demographics Breakdown"
NÚMEROS: $5,000-$10,000 capital disponível, Algumas horas por semana
VISUAL: Lista de características demográficas ideais, breakdown de perfil de cliente qualificado
[08:12] And a lot of people will list things like okay well they're in in his case it's well they're working a stable job they have at least you know $5,000 to $10,000 laying around that they can invest either in cash or on a credit card that they're comfortable paying off and and you know not going to go into debt over.
[08:31] They have a couple hours a week and you know they're looking to get some additional income, maybe quit their job and start selling on Amazon. And so that's how he that's how he articulates his ideal demographic.
[08:44] Okay. And I go, "Okay, anybody else?" And he's like, "Yeah, you know, there's a secondary demographic that might already have an Amazon store. They're kind of struggling. They're not making as much as they want to make.
[08:56] They've been trying it out for, you know, either a few weeks or a few months, maybe even a few years in some instances, and they're just not making enough. They don't know what they're doing wrong, and they need help.
[09:08] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Messaging Disconnect"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Comparação entre mensagem do anúncio vs. demografia ideal, destacando discrepância crítica
[09:10] So, then you go look at his front-end ads, okay? And here's here's what a lot of his front-end ads articulated. His front end ads were saying things like, "Hey, if you want to quit your nine-to-five job and you know, you want to start selling on Amazon, like, you know, press this button. Like, I'll help you out."
[09:28] Just to give you guys some perspective right off the bat with how huge of a difference there is. There's a massive discrepancy there. And I want to see if you guys can figure it out before I articulate it to you just to see where you guys are at.
[09:42] If his ideal demographic works a stable corporate job, high-paying, has 5 to $10,000 laying around, wants to get started on Amazon, where is the issue between his ideal demographic and what he's articulating in his ads?
[09:58] His ads saying, "Hey, if you want to quit your nine-to-five job, you want to start making money on Amazon, like you know, press this button." Who can see the problem right away? Feel free to unmute yourself and and give us some perspective. Anybody?
[10:13] Yeah. They don't necessarily want to quit their job right now. They just want to make a side hustle, additional income.
[10:20] Bingo. This is the This is a huge discrepancy once again that has to be addressed right away. And think about it. Blake hit it right on the nose.
[10:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Geographic Context Example"
NÚMEROS: $6,000-$12,000/mês aluguel (mínimo: $4,000), $150-$200 refeição para dois, 15k+/mês para 20%+ da área
VISUAL: Descrição de área de alto padrão, perfil de moradores estáveis com empregos corporativos
[10:32] And the area where I'm at right now where I got this second house, these are people who have high-paying stable jobs, some entrepreneurs, some people who are like there's a lot of people who are in the finance industry that live in this specific area.
[10:47] And these are people who live in like town homes or some of them live in apartments. The average rent here at the lowest is like 6,000 to $12,000 a month. Maybe there's a few spotty places that you can live for like 4K a month.
[11:01] There's people around here. There's, believe it or not, about 20 plus% of this entire area. It's like 15k plus a month for nothing crazy. I mean, it's just a nice neighborhood.
[11:12] To be clear though, these people, they have like maybe 1 to two cars, you know, and they're very active people. They're in a very walkable neighborhood. There's there's great food.
[11:22] Every time I've gone out here, just for a basic meal for two people, it's like 150, maybe sometimes even $200 depending on where you go. Could be a little less, you know, maybe maybe at the lowest like a hundred bucks.
[11:34] These people have stable lives. There's tons of families. There's there's just a lot of people here that are like young adults that are in the early stages of like family life or like right on the verge of transitioning to family life.
[11:47] And these people like when you look at them, it gives me a lot of perspective as a marketer. None of them want to just upend their entire life, stop what they're doing, quit their job, go all-in from a guy they saw in an ad, you know, trust his word that they're going to be able to quit their job and start making money on Amazon.
[12:08] Like, that's just not appealing to to that actual demographic that he's attempting to sell to.
[12:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Conditions of the Demographic"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Framework central - destaque para "CONDITIONS" como elemento mais importante em messaging
[12:17] So when you look at your ideal demographic, the second key category that you have to break down and focus on is called the conditions of the demographic.
[12:27] Okay, the conditions of the demographic that you are selling to are what is most important to work into your advertising messaging and into your funnel messaging and what your salespeople will talk about to that specific lead.
[12:42] Okay. The conditions of the demographic are one of the most undermined communication tactics in mastering messaging.
[12:51] Okay, I'll give you some examples with that exact one I just articulated to you. An ideal scenario based on the conditions of the demographic of, okay, I work a corporate job. I have a stable life.
[13:03] I just want to make some extra money on the side with an Amazon business. I don't want it to take too long because I don't want it to take time away from my family. They're not looking to lower their quality of life.
[13:15] If they're looking to improve their quality of life. They don't want to risk everything they've got going because they're in a great place. They want to put themselves into a better position.
[13:25] And if and only if eventually the Amazon business is long-term enough, exceeds or makes the same amount as their existing job. And if they don't like their job, maybe it gives them the option to quit.
[13:37] But that's probably extremely low on the priority list in terms of conditions of this individual's life that they're looking for inside of getting started with an Amazon business in this case.
[13:48] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Corrected Ad Messaging Example"
NÚMEROS: 2-3 horas/semana, $5,000-$10,000 investimento, 45 minutos para consumir oferta
VISUAL: Exemplo de copy corrigido, messaging alinhado com condições do público-alvo
[13:51] So, to be clear, I told Andrew, I'm like, try an ad like this. Try to say something along the lines of, "Listen, if you're a corporate professional, you love your job, you want to make some extra money on the side, you don't want to lower your quality of life.
[14:06] You're just looking for some extra money that's going to take you at most two hours, maybe three hours a week. It's going to give you an opportunity to go out there and have some additional income on a month-over-month basis, give you some additional spending cash, and it's not going to take an excessive amount of time.
[14:23] Got a perfect opportunity for you to get started selling on the number one e-commerce platform on earth, Amazon.com. I have put together an offer for you that will show you how the opportunity works, why it works great for people like you.
[14:36] And I'll demonstrate to you why it's only going to take about two to three hours a week max. Might even take less. I want to sell you on the idea and the opportunity in this free offer I've put together for you.
[14:47] It won't take you more than 45 minutes to consume this. And you can come back to it if you can't consume it all at once. I just want to disclose to take advantage of the opportunity after you're sold on it.
[14:58] You're likely going to need anywhere from about 5,000 at the lowest to about $10,000 to invest into this opportunity to get started on Amazon. But I'll show you how it works free of charge so you can see if it's the right opportunity for you. Press the button below and get started.
[15:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Ideal Conditions Framework"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Lista de condições ideais: melhorar qualidade de vida, riscos calculados, estabilidade
[15:17] Now to be clear that articulates the ideal conditions. Okay, once again the ideal conditions just to highlight them. I want to improve my quality of life not take away from it.
[15:28] I don't want to take substantial risk. I want to take calculated smart risks. As people age, they take less and less big risks. They typically take smarter and more calculated risks because they have more responsibility they take on as they continue to age.
[15:44] So, if you're targeting younger demographics, these people don't have a lot to risk and lose. They can take big risks because they're not really that big to them.
[15:53] You know, they're not really they're really, you know, putting putting everything on the board to lose in the instance that things go wrong for them.
[16:02] Versus, if I target like 40 to 60 year old men or women, dude, they're deep into their adulthood at that point. They likely have a family. They likely have mortgages or or or multiple ongoing fixed expenses.
[16:16] They likely, if they don't have a business, have families, you know, in most instances, depending on who you're targeting, of course. But regardless, once again, as people age, you've got to remove the risks being scary and make it sound simple and make it sound doable and make it sound like it doesn't take a substantial amount of time.
[16:36] Most people also are deep into a long-term concrete habitual set of behaviors as they continue to age as well. Most people hate change as they continue to get older and they don't want to upend their entire life for substantial change. They want stability.
[16:52] Okay, these are examples of conditions. And when I recognize I I once again I first want to define my ideal demographic and then I want to ideally articulate the conditions that that demographic wants to be in or are currently in.
[17:08] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Bridge Concept"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Conceito visual de ponte conectando situação atual a resultado desejado, baseado em condições ideais
[17:10] So I have to be able to successfully bridge an individual that I am talking to from where they are currently at with their current conditions and I have to be able to bridge them successfully based on the ideal scenario for them to get to the other side.
[17:25] And then I have to articulate the other side also as an ideal condition that they desire.
[17:31] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "James & Blake - Alcohol Program Case Study"
NÚMEROS: 40% querem parar temporariamente (não para sempre)
VISUAL: Exemplo de programa de cessação de álcool, breakdown de diferentes demografias de clientes
[17:33] So, to be clear, we'll use we'll use James and Blake on the call here. They have a program where they're they're selling people on cutting out alcohol completely.
[17:43] Now, to be clear, James, Blake, when you guys unmute yourself, how often do you get people who say to you, "Yeah, I don't want to stop drinking forever. You know, I just want to show myself I can stop, you know, get it under control period of time and then, you know, maybe I'll start after that. I just want the choice."
[18:01] It's about a lot. It's about 40%.
[18:05] Yeah. So, so think about that, right? So, it's like in terms of messaging, if you have that pie chart that I always talk about when it comes to the majority hook, you have 40% almost half that are telling you, well, I don't want to quit forever.
[18:21] I just, you know, I I want to be able to essentially stop for a sustained period of time and just, you know, I think I need some help to be able to do that. I don't think I can do that part on my own.
[18:33] But, you know, if you just sit here and consistently sell me that I have to quit forever and that's the entire messaging in your ads on the landing page and inside of your your salespeople's process, you're going to turn a lot of those people away, right?
[18:47] Like you're going to have as an example Adrian as the gatekeeper who doesn't allow those people through. You're going to lose those people right there. Does that make sense?
[18:56] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Demographic Conditions Analysis"
NÚMEROS: Faixa etária: final dos 20s, 30s, 40s
VISUAL: Perfil psicográfico de bebedor social, não se vê como alcoólatra, estável, busca controle
[18:58] So, the conditions of that demographic, think about it like this. They are likely a stable individual who doesn't view themselves as being an alcoholic. They don't view themselves yet as having a lot of the health issues that eventually come along. They're likely younger.
[19:14] You know, they're probably in their late 20s, 30s, 40s would be my bet. They're people that just get a lot of benefit technically from drinking in their own eyes.
[19:23] You know, they probably go out, they have a social life. Maybe it's what takes off their edge. Maybe it's their sedation tactic. Maybe it's been their long-term habitual this is what takes my edge off type behavior, okay?
[19:36] That they've settled into for years or potentially even a decade plus. Okay? Well, telling that person, you know, all these hypothetical scenarios, like what was at the beginning of your VSSL, as an example.
[19:49] Hey, you're depressed, anxious, you know, you're unhealthy, like you're starting to see the long-term ramifications of your decision-making play out, and you know, it's it's negatively affecting your entire life, like you need to cut out alcohol right now.
[20:03] They look at that and they just think to themselves, well, that I mean, that's not me. It doesn't make sense to me.
[20:09] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Multiple Demographics Strategy"
NÚMEROS: 40% temporário, 30% problema sério, 20% outro, 5% alcoólatra severo
VISUAL: Breakdown de hooks por demografia, distribuição percentual de tipos de clientes
[20:11] So, what do you do? What do you do when you can recognize I have multiple demographics that I can sell to based on messaging? Okay, so here's an ideal scenario that ends up playing out very well if you can pull this off.
[20:25] Okay, you have different demographics that we can recognize from the hooks. Okay, just like what they just said, they have over 40% of their ideal potential customers that are saying, "I just want to temporarily cut out drinking.
[20:38] I don't really know if I want to do it forever, you know, but hey, I'd give it a shot. I'd appreciate your help doing that." And then what's the other demographic?
[20:46] Well, I mean, to be fair, they could have a demographic that's, let's call it, you know, maybe 30% that is in the condition that they talk about at the start of their main VSSL that they drive 100% of their traffic to.
[21:00] So right now once again regardless of okay I have these people here who do have a big problem with alcohol who do struggle maybe I have an even smaller demographic that is like a raging alcoholic that can't go to bed without drinking that's got like the tremors you know.
[21:16] And then I have like these other demographics like maybe one of them's like 20% you know maybe one of them's like 5% simply put these are the hooks and we've talked about this before as a group.
[21:27] There's just demographics that you know you're going to appeal to based on what you say. Okay.
[21:33] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Common Mistake"
NÚMEROS: 100% do tráfego → mesma landing page
VISUAL: Diagrama mostrando múltiplos hooks convergindo para página única, destacando ineficiência
[21:35] Now, here's the thing. A lot of people will do this in a lax-a-dasical way where on the front end in their ads specifically, they'll take these hooks, okay, and they'll work them into their advertising efforts.
[21:48] But then the mistake is is they'll take 100% of these people and they'll push them to the exact same landing page. There is no difference in the messaging on the landing page.
[21:59] The landing page is geared towards technically a mix of everybody in most instances. Like sometimes you'll have guys that say things like, "Oh, I've got all these demographics covered. I say it in my VSSL."
[22:11] Well, as we know, I mean, in almost all instances, this is the most efficient way to do things. This is the least time and effort way to do things. And that's not always a that's not always a bad thing, to be fair.
[22:24] I mean, there's plenty of people in this very group who are at hundreds of thousands of dollars a month doing this exact process where the ad hooks are broke down and diversified well and you are successfully communicating the contextual situation of your ideal demographics, but then you drive them all to the same page.
[22:42] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Advanced Strategy: Multiple Landing Pages"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Diagrama de múltiplos hooks conectados a landing pages específicas, estratégia de scaling avançada
[22:44] Okay. What what isn't what is a really good idea to test especially when you're scaling is to have multiple variations. Okay.
[22:53] So imagine as an example for your for your demographic that you talked about that has you know 40% of your people saying well you know I don't know if I want to I want to cut out drinking forever.
[23:04] I don't really view myself as like anxious or you know depressed or you know like I'm not I'm not unhealthy. I mean I'm very active as a matter of fact like I go to the gym you know two to three times a week.
[23:16] I walk every day you know I'm I'm not fat. You know what I mean? I actually when I flex in the mirror I I I can see muscles you know like I feel good.
[23:25] I go to the doctor, I do my blood work, they tell me I'm healthy. But, you know, I do admit I don't want to become reliant on alcohol.
[23:33] And I am noticing in my lifestyle that when I go out, I'm always drinking or when I have friends over, I always open a bottle of wine or, you know, just to be social.
[23:43] I'm recognizing, okay, I'm I'm pretty reliant on alcohol, but I'm not seeing the negative ramifications of that yet. I can just recognize how that can turn into a problem.
[23:53] I would love somebody's help who can help me get control of that and maybe show me other ways to swap that out, you know, have fun in different ways, be able to live a little better, you know, and and just be able to improve my quality of life without being reliant on alcohol.
[24:09] Well, that's completely different in comparison to how this current page that 100% of the traffic goes to is articulating the problem now, is it not? You know, so think about this.
[24:21] When I when I just take especially when you recognize like Blake says 40% of the overall traffic coming in is saying that well having an entire landing page dedicated to that individual would help a lot.
[24:34] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "CRM Tagging & Sales Team Alignment"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Sistema de tagging por fonte de tráfego, integração entre landing pages e CRM para contexto de vendas
[24:36] And then in addition to that, right, well, why do I need to separate the landing page outside of just getting the messaging separated? Now, this is this is where you get into some advanced stuff. Okay, this is where you really start seeing the effects of this positively play into your favor as a company.
[24:53] You then take those landing pages and you have the unique tools that are embedded into those pages that collect the data. Whether it's the opt-in form, the application on the page, the scheduler tool on the page.
[25:04] You have the tools that are now separated per type of messaging that give you the ability to tag in your CRM this is where that person came from, what they responded to.
[25:16] Now, to be clear, this helps tremendously when it comes to the salespeople being able to talk to the person on the phone about what they came in to solve.
[25:26] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Adrian's Lead Qualification System"
NÚMEROS: 8-10 (alta urgência), 4-7 ou 5-7 (média urgência), "Rubbish" (baixa urgência)
VISUAL: Sistema de classificação de leads do sales manager, problema de categorização inadequada
[25:28] So, to be clear, I talked to to to James and Blake and their sales manager, Adrian, on Wednesday, and you know, we recognized very clearly that the the sales manager was taking what was talked about, okay?
[25:42] He's taking leads that are coming in and he's saying, "Well, there's people who are very like ready to go, you know, who who recognize they have this problem with drinking and, you know, they're ready to buy." The the sales manager calls those like eight to tens. Okay.
[25:57] And then he says there's these people who are like, you know, lower interest levels. He called them I think like five like four to sevens or like five to sevens.
[26:06] And then he called the other low-interest demographic rubbish. Okay. And the low-interest demographic being people who didn't really view themselves as high priority for cutting out alcohol.
[26:18] You know, they didn't have a lot of urgency is what the salesperson gave feedback on. And he called these people rubbish. That's what he labeled them as.
[26:27] When in reality, it's like those rubbish leads when you simply have the sales team adapted to the fact that there's a lower level of interest or a lower level of urgency. That can be incredible for the salesperson to know. So, they adapt their sales process to it.
[26:43] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Shape-Sorting Analogy"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Analogia do brinquedo infantil de formas geométricas, tentando forçar peças erradas em buracos errados
[26:45] Now add a layer to that which is just well what messaging did that individual respond to? Because their urgency might not come from cutting out alcohol forever if they're all forced into the same hole.
[26:56] It's like that game we all played when we were a little kid where we had little wood pieces that were shaped like a cylinder or a cube or a triangle and you try to take every triangle and put it in the cylinder hole, you know?
[27:10] You try to take the rectangle and put it in the circle hole. It's like the the pieces don't fit when you try to force everything through the the shape that is wrong. Okay.
[27:21] So, when we add messaging to what our sales team can leverage for an individual, imagine once again the people respond to that hook. They go to a page that's specific to that hook.
[27:32] And then once again that page labels them as this person is a social drinker who recognizes they might have a problem in the future. They don't think they have a problem now, but they think they might in the future.
[27:44] And the salesperson adapts their sales process to that specific issue. Salesperson adapts their sales process to talk to that person based on their conditions that we talked about.
[27:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Fundamental Problem"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Empresa forçando projeção de condições ideais nos leads, negando oportunidade baseada em critérios errados
[27:57] Remember, every company has an ideal demographic and that ideal demographic has an ideal set of conditions. Okay? And once again, they came through the funnel to stop drinking.
[28:08] For the sales team to just say, "Oh, they're all rubbish." Like those people aren't probable to close. That just doesn't make sense. It doesn't make sense. They obviously came through the funnel for a reason.
[28:19] In their head, they've successfully taken what you've presented to them and concluded, "Yeah, this might be worth talking to these guys. You know, maybe I should cut out alcohol."
[28:29] But just because the sales team then says, "Oh, these people don't have excessive levels of urgency. These people aren't in a position where they're ready to buy right now. I'm not going to let them talk to a closer." Makes no sense.
[28:42] The real issue there is is that the company has a projection that they're forcing on to the leads for what conditions they should be in to buy. Kind of selfish if you think about it.
[28:54] You know, you're denying the opportunity for people who want to stop drinking to buy in that example because they didn't want to buy the way you tried to force them to buy.
[29:04] They had different reasons that you as a company didn't identify were the right fit and so you chose for them to cut them out.
[29:13] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Ethical Responsibility"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Conceito de responsabilidade ética em vendas, consequências de não vender para quem precisa
[29:15] Just to put that in perspective as a company, it's like that book that you read that gives you the ability to pick what happens next and then you go to that next chapter.
[29:24] You kind of forced the hand for that individual. You might lead them to a point in their life where they don't get the results that you could have provided for them and they do end up in a dark place as a result of not selling them now because you didn't you didn't try to, you know.
[29:39] And think about that for any offer. If you guys most of you here have make money offers or you work with people who have make money offers or save time offers or in this case solve problem offers.
[29:51] When you don't allow your business to sell to these individuals, it's a big problem. You know, you're you're essentially leading them to a darker place.
[30:01] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Complete Framework Recap"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Framework completo: Demographics → Conditions → Bridge (Ads + Funnels + Sales)
[30:03] So, to be clear, and let me just reiterate real quick, it all starts with identifying our ideal demographic. When we identify our ideal demographic, we can start to get a picture. It's just the start though.
[30:16] We can start to get a picture of what we then need to understand, which is the conditions of these people's lives.
[30:22] Like Andrew with his Amazon business. I want to sell to corporate professionals who want to potentially quit their job, but in reality, they just want to make more money. They want to improve their quality of life. They just want more spending cash.
[30:36] The conditions to transition. We then have to we have to understand what's called the bridge. Okay? Demographic traits, conditions of that person's current life. And the bridge being what actually gets them across to purchasing from us.
[30:52] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Bridge Definition"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Definição clara da ponte: comunicar transição de estado atual para resultado desejado da forma que ELES querem
[30:54] The bridge is how we communicate effectively the transition from where they are now to getting to the other side where they get the result we're after helping them get in a way that they want to get it. That's all the bridge is.
[31:08] The bridge to be clear once again is articulating how we get them the result in a way that they want to get it.
[31:15] When we successfully articulate the conditions of our ideal demographic now and we articulate to them how we're going to get them to the result that we want to get for them in a way that they want to get it, we're going to sell a fuckload more people than we are now.
[31:29] And to be clear, as we've recognized, there are different conditions and different demographics. Therefore, there are different bridges.
[31:37] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Bridge Components"
NÚMEROS: 3 partes
VISUAL: Visualização da ponte em 3 componentes: Ads (início) → Funnels (meio) → Sales/CTA (fim)
[31:39] What is our bridge tactically? To be clear, our bridge is our advertising messaging and our funnel messaging. And the last part of the bridge, if I broke it down in a in a visual for you guys, the last part of the bridge is the sales team or that final call to action.
[31:55] So once again, where they are now, where we want them to go. The bridge is broke down in three parts. The ads, the funnels, and the sales team.
[32:04] And this is also once again in some instances not a sales team. This is like the end of your webinar where you're converting traffic or the end of the challenge where you're you where you're communicating effectively.
[32:15] So to be clear, starting point them actually buying. They have to be able to see it. Okay.
[32:21] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Rickety Bridge Analogy"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Analogia cinematográfica de ponte instável, pessoa recusando atravessar por parecer insegura
[32:23] How many times have you guys seen a movie, maybe you've even been hiking before and you've done this yourself. You you have a character that approaches a bridge, a literal bridge, and the bridge looks shaky and messed up and it looks like you could fall through it or it looks rickety, you know, just doesn't look safe.
[32:41] What's the probability that that character just says, "Fuck that. I'm not going across that bridge, dude. I'm gonna find another way." That's what happens all the time.
[32:49] Like, as an example, James Blake, for the 40% of your demographic that says, "Yeah, I mean, you know, I'm a social drinker. I don't have like a ton of urgency to cut out alcohol, but you know, I would I can see it being a problem one day."
[33:02] And then your sales team, like maybe maybe the ads work effectively because they brought them into the funnel and then obviously the funnel gets them in.
[33:10] Like there's something they identify with to get all the way to the sales team, but then they get to the sales team and the sales team's part of the bridge looks like rickety and messed up and they don't end up getting to the other side of it or vice versa.
[33:23] A lot of the times it's well, they start here, they just look at the bridge and the ads, they're completely off. There's there's nothing in the ads that say, "Yeah, that's for me." And they just turn around and they go back the other way and they try to find another bridge.
[33:36] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Three-Part Alignment"
NÚMEROS: 3 componentes
VISUAL: Ênfase na necessidade de alinhamento perfeito entre ads, funnels e sales para completar a ponte
[33:38] My point being these three components. Okay, now take what I talked about earlier once again with the hooks. Okay, we have our majority hooks, we have our minority hooks, we have all kinds of hooks that we can leverage throughout our process that create individual opportunities to convert traffic.
[33:55] We have our funnels, our landing pages that we then push these demographics to and that ideally repeat the messaging from what was said in our hooks.
[34:04] And it's the same logic I talked about before. If we then have a sales team, we have a closer who's on the other end of the phone that says, "Oh, sorry, this isn't for you." or just communicates a completely different message.
[34:16] Our bridge is broken and we will not get people at scale successfully to the other side. It is a three-part process that has to be mastered and that has to be aligned for the bridge to be completed. Okay.
[34:29] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Conditions Are Key"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Reforço final: condições são o maior componente para fazer o processo de 3 partes funcionar
[34:31] Now, to be clear once again, conditions are the biggest part of making this three-part process work. What are the ideal scenarios? What are the ideal circumstances? What are the ideal literal conditions of the process to get from A to B?
[34:46] If I tell people once again, just like Andrew was trying to say, "Oh yeah, my ideal demographic are people working corporate jobs. They're professionals. They have stable lives. I want to tell them to quit their nine-to-five jobs so they can start an Amazon business." Makes no sense.
[35:03] The first step in the ads is not a condition that the user agrees with. They turn around. They don't go across the bridge.
[35:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Correct Conditions Messaging Example"
NÚMEROS: 2-3 horas/semana, $5-$10,000
VISUAL: Exemplo extenso de messaging correto alinhado com todas as condições do público-alvo
[35:12] If the ads say, "Look, you're working a corporate job. You love your job. Nothing's wrong. You know, you just want to make some extra cash on the side. You want to have some extra spending money. You don't want to lower your quality of life in the process of acquiring a new skill.
[35:27] You want to make sure that you still have time to do everything you love. I have an opportunity that's going to take you less than two to three hours a week to get started with selling on the number one e-commerce platform on Earth, Amazon.com.
[35:40] It's not going to require you to buy inventory upfront. There's plenty of ways around that. It's going to cost you about $5 to $10,000 once you're sold on the opportunity.
[35:49] And what I'm going to do for you, I'm going to show you exactly how you can get started with an Amazon business in under two to three hours a week. I'm going to show you why the opportunity makes sense.
[35:59] And I'm going to show you how much money it can make you in that two to three hours a week. I'm going to make sure that you understand it's not going to lower your quality of life at all. It's going to add to your quality of life.
[36:11] It's going to give you more options because you're going to have more money. And eventually one day maybe it's making enough as much as your job or maybe even more where you're going to get the opportunity to have more freedom in your life and walk away from your current current job. Or maybe just keep both. It's up to you.
[36:28] Of course, if you are a busy professional looking to add to your quality of life with some additional income and spending power, I'd love for you to press the button. Let me show you how Amazon works and why it's a great opportunity for you.
[36:41] The person sees that ad. They say, "Yeah, I agree with that." They then go to his funnel and his funnel articulates the same messaging in a little more depth. That's what the funnel's for. Ads are the hook. The funnel is the depth.
[36:55] They agree with the funnel as well. Similar messaging. They're sold on the opportunity. Now then they go talk to Andrew's sales team. The sales team gets to know the person. They're not hard selling. They feel cool. You know, they're relating to the individual.
[37:09] And they they successfully get the person sold. Okay, that'd be an ideal scenario for him in that case. And once again, all three steps have to align for the person to cross the bridge.
[37:20] And and once again, the biggest problem for Andrew right now in his case for his Amazon business was he was telling people right off the rip, they needed to quit. That they just had to stop everything they're doing and just quit their job.
[37:33] So, okay, to be clear, anybody have questions on this so far? I want to make sure you guys understand this. This is very important. Feel free to unmute yourself and ask away if you got anything.
[37:43] Does anybody have any good examples? Does anybody see some obvious signs in their current processes that are messed up that aren't aligned where they need to break something out and make a contextual version of a landing page or maybe get the sales team on the same page as the funnel and the ads? Anybody got anything on that?
[38:02] Um, it's James here. We could we could be less stringent on the initial phone call in the beginning and make someone who was feeling feeling more positive about quitting alcohol make them feel more comfortable that it was for them.
[38:18] And so put them through to the second second range of calls. So we could do that.
[38:23] I'm understanding everything that you're saying and and at the same time given I've been doing this and talking to prospective clients for years it's it's been very rare in my experience that in my offer if they don't have pain they they don't they don't enroll enroll.
[38:40] If they don't have pain, they don't enroll. So, in this scenario where you were saying be more open to them, right? You're like, well, maybe they want to just, you know, quit for a little while.
[38:51] And it seems to me that they're considering that more like a fun little challenge, something just to get their health in order. And therefore they wouldn't they in their own mind would not justify investing 15,000 $25,000 to do so.
[39:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── NÚMEROS: $15,000-$25,000 investimento do programa
VISUAL: Objeção de preço vs. nível de urgência percebida
[39:07] Now I'm not I'm not just basing that on my own conviction. I'm basing it on having spoken to, you know, hundreds of people over the years. So how would you reconcile that?
[39:17] Like I get what you're putting down and at the same time practically I don't feel that has I don't feel what you are now suggesting we do has traditionally led to people enrolling.
[39:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Ferrari FF Story"
NÚMEROS: 2,200 carros no mundo, $70,000 cotação inicial da Ferrari, $25-28k estimativa do mecânico, $7,000 para shocks traseiros ($3k cada peça + $1k mão de obra), Custo futuro: $13-14,000, $3,400 transporte ida e volta
VISUAL: História detalhada de reparo de carro como analogia de vendas, diagnóstico completo vs. problema percebido
[39:30] So, I have I have two Ferraris and one of them is called a Ferrari FF and just recently when I was driving my FF like I don't know like two months ago, I got some kind of like I don't know what it was kind of felt like the clutch was disengaging when I was ripping around in it.
[39:47] I would be in first gear punching the gas like you should in any nice car and it would it would kind of like disengage for a second and then just like snap right back into into gear.
[39:57] And you know, right away I recognized I'm like, "All right, well obviously I got some kind of transmission issue here." I took it over to Ferrari and Ferrari quotes me a really high amount. They quoted me like $70,000 to get this fixed.
[40:11] So I there's only like 2,200 of these cars that exist on Earth. And and thankfully I'm in a Facebook group with almost all the owners. So I go in there and I'm like, "Hey guys, like I just got quoted 70 GS by Ferrari. Like what should I do?"
[40:25] I I had a lot of pain to be clear to want to fix this specific issue. There ends up being a guy in Texas. His name's Lux Tech. And I end up shipping the car from here in Miami to Texas to get that fixed.
[40:39] And soon as he gets the car, he just does a full-blown diagnosis. Now, keep in mind, I'm prepared to want to spend money on the gearbox specifically. Like, I need to fix the transmission.
[40:51] And this guy rebuilds the transmissions in entirety for like 15k, maybe like 18k most, depending on how how much is messed up.
[40:59] So after his full diagnosis, he comes back with a wrap sheet of stuff James. Like a wrap sheet. Like he's like, "Okay, the intake manifold is leaking oil onto the front transmission."
[41:12] So the front transmission is not actually messed up at all. It's just actually you have an intake manifold leak that needs fixed. He goes, "The car just needs general service. The back gearbox, there's two transmissions in the car."
[41:24] He goes, "The back gearbox is actually the one that's messed up that's causing your issue." He goes, "You definitely need to fix that." He goes, "Your rear shocks, one of them's actually leaking. I need to fix both of those."
[41:36] And you know, I think he listed like two or three other things. And I go, "How much is all this going to cost?" And he goes, "Uh, he's a rough rough estimate. Probably like 25 grand, maybe like 28 grand."
[41:49] And I wasn't that shocked by that actually. I was like, "Oh, okay. That actually feels kind of fair given everything you listed."
[41:56] He goes, "Yeah, but let me run the numbers and like let me confirm with you for sure." And I go I I expressed to him, I'm like, "Look, ideally we keep it under 25K. I want to fix the main problems, you know, like make it drivable and like that's the main thing I want to solve."
[42:13] And he goes, "Uh, totally understand." He takes like two days. He comes back to me and he says, "Okay, so for for doing all of it under 25 grand, I can do literally everything besides the shocks.
[42:26] The shocks in the back cost 3K each just for the part." And he goes, "To be clear, like I'll be back there anyway. So if in terms of my cost to like fix it, that'd be the cheap part.
[42:37] Like I could probably fix it for I don't know, maybe like a thousand bucks. So you're looking at like seven grand to fix the rear shocks on top of the 25K."
[42:46] And I was like, "Well, were the rear shocks messed up before I sent it to you?" He goes, "Oh, yeah, for sure." And I go, "Well, to be fair, like when I was driving it around, like I didn't feel I didn't feel that way at all.
[42:59] I didn't have any kind of like mental association that my rear shocks were messed up. Like I would go over bumps and stuff and I wouldn't I wouldn't say they were fucked."
[43:08] And he was like, "Oh, yeah." He's like, "To me, it's so obvious, you know, like when I'm driving over them, you know, it's it like it makes a weird sound. It sounds like I can like feel like it's just a little more abrupt than it should be. Like the suspension feels more firm than it should be, simply put.
[43:25] And I was I was like, well, I really want to keep this under 25K, so I don't want to fix the rear shocks right now. Like, let's do that later.
[43:33] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Sales Opportunity"
NÚMEROS: $7,000 agora vs. $13-14,000 depois, $3,400 transporte adicional no futuro, $1,000 mão de obra agora vs. $3,000+ depois
VISUAL: Mecânico vendendo dor futura, fazendo cliente associar problema não percebido com consequências reais
[43:35] Now, here's the opportunity where he needs to sell me on the pain that I don't associate to my rear shocks or he loses out on the opportunity to fix that specific issue because I don't identify as having a rear shock problem.
[43:50] I don't from driving a car for almost a year that he says was a problem the entire time in any way, shape, or form think that there's an actual problem there. Okay?
[44:00] I could be driving around with, I don't know, one messed up rear shock and not even have known it. Okay? Now, this guy does some really smart stuff James. Okay?
[44:10] So, he goes, he sells me on the pain I'm going to have. Okay? I then can associate with the future pain from how he broke this down. Okay, so he goes, "Look, I totally get it. You want to be at 25 grand, okay? And an extra seven grand on top of that would put you at 32."
[44:28] And I just want to be clear. First of all, the first thing he articulates to me is that it's going to be more expensive in the future. He goes, "Dude, if I send this thing back to you and then you got to send it back to me," he goes, "This 3,400 round trip."
[44:42] So he goes, "We have 3,400 we have to add on to that cost in the future if you ever do want to solve the problem." He goes, "Two? I'm charging you a thousand bucks in labor because I'm already going to have the car pretty much taken apart because I got to drop the transmission out of it.
[44:58] So for me, it's not like I have to take a bunch of stuff off." He goes, "This an intricate car, man. It's a Ferrari. It's a lot of stuff that I have to take apart to get to that in the first place.
[45:09] So, my cost in the future is likely going to be upwards of like three grand as well rather than the $1,000 I could charge you now. And he goes, "The part cost is going to stay the same."
[45:19] He goes, "So, right now I could solve the problem for $7,000." He goes, "In the future, when you do want to solve the problem again, it's going to cost like 13 to $14,000."
[45:29] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Selling Future Pain"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Técnicas de venda: custo futuro, vazamento visível, som estranho, experiência degradada, segurança familiar
[45:31] He goes, "In addition to that, yeah, you might not identify that you have the issue. Do you like coming out to your car and seeing a small leak by one of your tires?" And I'm like, "Well, no. I I've never seen a small leak."
[45:45] He goes, "Well, do you think the problem gets better as it just sits there and not fixed, or do you think it gets worse?" And I'm like, "Dude, listen, James. I'm a status driven individual, okay?
[45:56] I don't ever want to come out to a car that has leaks underneath it when I pull out. I'd feel like a loser, okay? I'd feel like a loser driving a car around that even has a problem." And he starts to dig into that.
[46:09] So he goes, "Look, man. I don't want you coming out to your car like seeing a small puddle of trans I mean a a small puddle of uh what's what's what's the word I'm looking for here? uh the shock. I don't want you to see a small puddle of shock oil on the ground.
[46:26] And on top of that, man, like as you're driving this thing, it's going to make a weird sound when you go over bumps. Like, I don't want you hearing that. I don't want other people hearing that. I don't want you driving around the car knowing it has a problem. That just doesn't doesn't make the Ferrari experience good.
[46:42] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Conversion Moment"
NÚMEROS: $32,000 total final
VISUAL: Múltiplos pontos de dor convergindo, decisão de compra baseada em dor futura visualizada no presente
[46:44] Now, all these things are kind of coming full circle for me here, James. He's selling me on the additional cost I'm gonna have in the future. He's selling me on the fact that like I come out to a car with like a small little puddle there.
[46:57] He's selling me on the fact that like other people and me are going to hear the sound of this sound I don't even know if I've heard or not, you know? And on top of that, he then Cherry on the Sunday adds the experience of just knowing that something's wrong while I'm driving it.
[47:12] He goes, "Look, man, like you have a he" He goes, "I follow you on Instagram, Jeremy. I know you have a fiance. I know you have little dogs. Like what if you're driving somewhere like over to the beach or something or over to your other house.
[47:25] And like you know it does have an issue and you can't drive it anymore in the middle like you have your family in the car. I'm like yeah dude no you're right. I was like you're right you know go ahead go ahead and fix it. Like whatever I'll spend the 32 like it makes more sense.
[47:41] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Sales Lesson"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Lição principal: vender dor futura fazendo sentir no presente, não assumir que leads sem urgência óbvia não podem comprar
[47:43] And my point being he took a moment to sell me on the future and and make me feel it in the present. Okay. He took something that I literally did not in any way, shape, or form associate with.
[47:56] And made me associate it to like an experience that I'm gonna have. Not even an experience that I've had, an experience that I'm probable to have in the future. Okay?
[48:06] So when you talk to these people who are like, you know, treating it like a fun challenge to potentially get healthier and and you're you're talking about this disconnect between, you know, all these people you've talked to and essentially not being able to sell them on the idea that this is worth them putting the money towards and investing the time and effort into.
[48:26] I don't view it as a I don't view it as a they're not probable to buy. I just view it as a sales training disconnect, you know?
[48:34] I view it as there is some form of communication that you have failed to test that can get those people converted when you successfully associate the future pain of their decision-making to today in ways that are agreeable to them.
[48:50] Cuz I I don't disagree with what the guy said. Everything that guy said to me about the future of my vehicle, I can totally relate to. I understand it and I can like picture a future where I have that experience. Okay?
[49:03] It is a reality. It's not like it's not like a probable future that I can avoid. It is like an an inevitable future of of feelings that I will experience. Okay.
[49:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Sales Team Adaptation Required"
NÚMEROS: 40% do tráfego sendo desperdiçado
VISUAL: Necessidade de treinar setters para fazer perguntas que conectem dor futura com presente
[49:17] So, to be clear, when you assume going into the sale that there's no probability that they're likely to purchase and you just assume that, obviously, you're not going to attempt to communicate and you're not going to attempt to try to persuade them.
[49:31] Otherwise, I would just encourage you continue to to adjust the persuasion tactics that you're deploying in an attempt to see which one's going to land successfully with that demographic since it seems like a minority majority of your demographic coming through is that type of buyer.
[49:47] Like if you get 40% upwards of those people that come through, that's just a lot of money lost. Does that make sense?
[49:54] Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. Um, so practically speaking, if we were to, let's just say we were to test that, the quickest way to test without going off and building a whole bunch of other landing pages because we're already getting 40% of people who are booking calls booking.
[50:11] Would be just to change the messaging on those first sales call, the first triage call.
[50:16] Yeah, that's correct. You have to be able to get those triage setters to be able to set the tone that there's something they can associate with in terms of future pain they can experience now, you know?
[50:28] Yeah. And it has to be real. That's the most important part I want to make sure I express to you is it's not like a made-up pain. It's something once again just based on future conditions that they can identify with and bringing them into right now.
[50:43] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Sample Sales Questions"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Exemplos de perguntas para descobrir e amplificar dor futura: timing, controle, facilidade de resolver agora vs. depois
[50:45] Um, look, I'll give you a great example, James, as a question you can have these setters ask. Uh, James, do you think it's going to be easier to get control of this type of problem now?
[50:56] Or do you think, God forbid that this gets out of control, becomes something that you do actually struggle with in the future, or maybe even one of your friends or family members struggles with in the future.
[51:07] Wouldn't it be better to have the power internally to just flip the switch to stop than have to deal with it when it's getting out of control?
[51:15] Like, when do you think it's easier to solve a drug problem? After the person's already addicted to the drug or before they get addicted to drugs?
[51:23] Like, yeah, you're healthy right now, man. Do you think you're going to have as much motivation to solve the problem when you start to get unhealthy?
[51:30] You know, these would be examples of like some of the questions I'd be thinking with to try to see if I can get like a like a deeper connection that leads to the next set of questions that are circumstantial based. Does that make sense?
[51:43] I'm going to just try to start to dig into the pain like on a on like a surface level. And once I find one, I'm drilling down on that hard.
[51:52] I'm gonna make them articulate to me when I find that surface level question that hits. I'm gonna make them start like articulating how it would be in the future and I'm going to make them experience it now.
[52:03] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Movie Emotion Analogy"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Como filmes fazem chorar por situações não reais - mesma técnica aplicada em vendas
[52:05] You ever watch a movie and get sad? You know, like you really connect with the characters and you're just like, "Fuck, dude." You know, it just doesn't feel good watching that right now.
[52:14] It's like that's what you're trying to do. Humans are great at being able to like tell our self story and experience emotion with things that like literally aren't even happening.
[52:23] You know, we have to be able to connect with that future. That's all it is. There's just a disconnect in your setters in this case. Being able to drill down with an individual and make them feel it. That's all.
[52:35] Yeah. The pursuit of happiness, dude. Well, we can Yeah, we can we can test it. We We will test it.
[52:43] Yeah, because you're getting them right now. Think about it. Like why would somebody respond to your current think about your current VSSL once again that we talked about on Wednesday.
[52:53] It starts off in the first several seconds just saying anxious, depressed, like messed up. You know, it's like why would somebody who who identifies as, oh, this is just kind of a casual thing. You know, I want to treat it like a challenge to stop drinking. Why would they respond to that type of funnel?
[53:09] Why would they respond to the way your current ads are laid out if they don't in some way, shape, or form identify with that? Does that make sense?
[53:17] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Hidden Self-Recognition"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Conceito de versão escondida de si mesmo que se identifica com a mensagem, mesmo negando conscientemente
[53:19] There's some version of them that sees that in themselves. That's what I'm trying to tell you. Yeah. When I talk to people, I'll test I'll test I I I'm willing to test everything.
[53:30] I'll test it even despite my skepticism based on my experience. It's fine. We can we can we can test it and see what happens. I'm open to being wrong about my conviction. So that's fine.
[53:42] I I agree with you on on being skeptical about it to be fair. I'm more so just saying from the perspective of what the setters need to be thinking.
[53:51] Like as long as the setters are aware, this person like if you just have your setters freshly go through the ads and the VSSL and say, "Look, man. I mean, this person responded to this and they got on the call.
[54:03] Just because they don't identify in their current state, who the wants to identify like that? Think about it like that. Who wants to identify as like, "Yeah, this is my current state."
[54:14] Like, nobody who's even in that state wants to typically identify with that state, you know? We want to identify with what we want to be.
[54:22] So, my point being, it's like it's the setter's job to simply put just kind of ask some of those questions that just generate the the conversation to get away from what we want to identify with to that version of them that clearly exists in their head. Dude, that's all I'm saying.
[54:38] There has to be a ver. There's no way you get human beings on the phone to talk about quitting drinking with the way you've articulated it in those VSSLs that you currently have running without them having some version of themselves in a particular moment in their life when they responded to it that said, "Oh yeah, I'm just here to like go happily talk about cutting out drinking in in like a like a fun challenge kind of way."
[55:02] There's a version of them, dude, that's thinking to themselves like, "I got a messed up version of me inside of me that I think's going to come out one day, you know, or like I can see that path clearly."
[55:13] But then, you know, they're going to just open up and talk about that. The right questions get people to open up and talk about those things, you know what I mean?
[55:21] Yeah, I got it. Okay. All right. Cool. All right. Thank you. No problem.
[55:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Final Exercise & Action Items"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Exercício de auditoria: revisar ads, funnels e sales process buscando desconexões
[55:30] To be clear, does anybody else have any questions on on the messaging mastery here? Any other perspectives you guys want to add?
[55:38] I'll leverage what what what homeowner Nick talked about in the chat here, which is the movie The Pursuit of Happiness. It does such an incredible job at taking a current demographic and just demonstrating a bridge to you, you know.
[55:53] And showcasing the raw emotion that comes along the way, taking you on that path, making you feel a lot. Getting you to the other side successfully where the man comes out.
[56:03] You know, there's moments of hope and glimmer along the way. We're not making movies in our ads and in our funnels and in our sales processes by any means.
[56:12] But what we do have to consider is is the logic that movie directors use every time that they create a masterful movie, which is the same process we've just articulated here.
[56:23] It's they've successfully created a connected sequence of events for us to get that bridge to experience what the character is experiencing and to relate to it along the way.
[56:35] If you just take simply put a moment to reflect on your own process, I'd encourage that you at least as an exercise go through the ads, the funnel, and the sales process and see the disconnects for yourself.
[56:48] Try to get that third person perspective on your own processes or on your client's processes and just try to look for Andrew as an example.
[56:57] It was so obvious when you just look at it as well the conditions of a person who has a stable job like why in the would they have appeal?
[57:06] He's getting a bunch of unqualified people because to be clear, unqualified people who hate their job, who don't like their life, who would be happy to quit, they're pumped for the opportunity to quit their job and start doing anything else. And if that's Amazon, great.
[57:21] But to be clear, a qualified person for him, once again, it was such an obvious disconnect as soon as he looked at it from that third person perspective, getting this in to his head and just thinking with it.
[57:33] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Masterful Way"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Contraste entre forma fácil (listar demographics) vs. forma masterful (articular conditions)
[57:35] So once again, go through your funnels, go through your ads, go through your sales processes, see if you can see any disconnects. Try to think with conditions.
[57:44] And that's what you want to express. Don't just express demographic traits. Like say in your ads, hey, if you're a a corporate busy professional and you make this amount of money and you have this credit score, like that's that's the easy way to do it.
[57:59] The masterful way to do it, once again, is articulating the conditions of these individual. That's that's where the real money is.
[58:08] Okay, cool. If anybody has any follow-up questions, let me know. Um, to be clear, we have the mastermind this upcoming weekend. Looking forward to seeing everybody who's making it out.
[58:19] Um, for everybody who's not coming in person, we got the live streaming that'll be happening during the event, of course, and then we also record everything. So, we'll send that over.
[58:28] And, uh, yeah, pumped to see everybody. All right, guys. Talk soon.
📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO
NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS:
Alex (trader): $100 → $379,000 em 3 meses
38,000 registros no webinar
$7 milhões em vendas do webinar
Curso: $1,500-$3,000
Andrew (Amazon): $5,000-$10,000 investimento necessário
2-3 horas/semana tempo requerido
James & Blake: 40% querem parar temporariamente (não para sempre)
Programa álcool: $15,000-$25,000
Ferrari FF: apenas 2,200 no mundo
Cotação Ferrari: $70,000
Custo real reparo: $25-28k → $32k final
Shocks traseiros: $3k cada + $1k mão de obra = $7k agora vs. $13-14k depois
Transporte: $3,400 ida e volta
Área geográfica: $6-12k/mês aluguel (20%+ pagam $15k+)
Refeições: $150-200 para dois
ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS:
Framework de Messaging Mastery: Demographics → Conditions → Bridge
The Bridge (3 Componentes): Ads → Funnels → Sales Team/CTA
Hooks Strategy: Majority hooks vs. Minority hooks separados por landing pages
CRM Tagging: Etiquetar leads por mensagem que responderam para adaptar sales process
Lead Qualification: 8-10 (alta urgência), 4-7 (média), "Rubbish" (baixa) - todos vendáveis
Multiple Landing Pages: Uma para cada hook principal (40%+ do tráfego)
Sales Training: Fazer prospects sentirem dor futura no presente
Future Pain Selling: Associar consequências futuras inevitáveis com emoção presente
ESTRUTURA:
Messaging aplica-se a: Ads, Funnels (VSSLs, headlines, body copy), Email/SMS automation, Sales team
Ideal Demographic = Traits + Conditions
Conditions > Demographics para messaging effectiveness
Bridge = Como comunicar transição do estado atual para resultado desejado DA FORMA que eles querem
Alignment crítico: Se um dos 3 componentes da ponte falha, conversão falha
Evitar forçar todos os leads pelo mesmo funil (analogia do brinquedo de formas)
DADOS FINANCEIROS:
Alex trader: $7M de 38k registros = ~$184 por registro
ROI de messaging correto: "fuckload more sales"
Custo de não vender para 40% do tráfego: "lot of money lost"
Exemplo Ferrari: Upsell de $7k para $32k através de future pain selling
LIÇÕES PRINCIPAIS:
Conditions > Demographics: Articular condições ideais de vida, não apenas traits demográficos
The Bridge Must Be Complete: Ads, Funnels e Sales devem estar 100% alinhados
Don't Force Fit: Leads diferentes precisam de mensagens diferentes (40% querem parar temporariamente vs. para sempre)
Future Pain Technique: Fazer prospects sentirem consequências futuras agora
Status Quo Preservation: Pessoas estáveis não querem arriscar tudo - querem melhorar, não destruir
Age = Risk Aversion: Quanto mais velhos, menos riscos grandes e mais riscos calculados
Nobody Wants to Identify with Pain: Mesmo quem tem o problema não quer admitir no estado atual
Sales Training Gap: "Rubbish leads" são apenas leads que precisam de abordagem diferente
Ethical Responsibility: Não vender para quem precisa os leva para lugar mais escuro
DURAÇÃO: 58:28 FORMATO: Training call ao vivo com exemplos de clientes reais e role-play
